description: Phase-1 gate-closing replication of puzzle_002 (7x7 hard), 4 toolsets meta-off, n=3, via the resumable budget-capped run_plan over multiple 5h windows.
motivation: The pre-committed Phase-1 decision: do the hard-puzzle cost ratios separate with non-overlapping CIs (signal real) or collapse (signal dead)?
formal_results: cost mu+-sigma (n=3): none $4.75+-0.94; fine $4.45+-0.92; medium $3.29+-0.62; coarse $2.59+-0.49. coarse/none = 0.55x; 1-sigma CIs none [3.81,5.69] vs coarse [2.10,3.09] - a $0.72 gap, non-overlapping. none solves in 2-3 calls yet is priciest; fine 121 calls.
results_description: On the hard puzzle the granularity cost ordering is statistically real: coarse is ~half the cost of none with separated CIs, inverting the easy-puzzle null result.
conclusions: Phase-1 gate PASS. The granularity cost-benefit is real but conditional on hard-enough puzzles; also the first clean evidence of the calls/cost decoupling (none 2 calls costs more than fine 121 calls).
report: Full window-by-window detail for the Phase-1 gate (puzzle_002, n=3 per toolset, windows W1-W7), the pre-committed decision rule, and the gate-PASS verdict (coarse/none = 0.55x, non-overlapping 1-sigma CIs) is in the stream-level report (source: notes/2026-05-18_aaai_plan_and_meta_confound.md, section "Phase-1 gate verdict").
methodology: Pre-registered Phase-1 gate: puzzle_002 (7x7 hard) at n=3 per toolset, meta-off, with the decision rule fixed in advance (non-overlapping 1-sigma coarse/none CIs => signal real). Run via the resumable, budget-capped harness/run_plan.py across multiple 5h windows; only solved trials counted. Opus 4.7, max_turns=200.
